不推荐没玩过同类型游戏的新人玩家入手，上手会非常折磨，需要适应游戏操作手感，地刺即死真的很恶心。游戏主要难点全在探图上，boss反而不难，并且难度曲线非常离谱，就前期那雪山和洪都大钟那完全是劝退新人，制作组设计时就不是给一般玩家玩的，相反到后期地图就又非常简单。而且隐藏内容很多，就比如血瓶和生命、蓝条上限都能给你藏起来不放主线道路上，很多要靠敲墙壁隐藏门里面，本来地图各种拦路设计就恶心,不知道制作组怎么想的。 反正我打完感觉是走完一场苦痛之旅，长舒一口气终于解脱了。第一遍打一半实在打不出去了，去搜攻略才发现我一堆东西没拿，血瓶上限都没几个，最后还是看着攻略玩下去的。如果你想挑战自己是个不错的选择，对于脾气爆点的可能要摔手柄了（ 总之美术风格还是挺独特的，很多小细节看得出制作组花了小心思，什么宗教方面很有考究，但玩法上我给不出很高评价，游戏给出负反馈太强了。

Totalmente recomendable, 8.75/10.  Un Metroidvania que mantiene las bases a un alto nivel acompañado de un estilo artístico único inspirado en un estilo Barroco bien oscuro estilo gótico con un pixel art precioso siendo sin lugar a duda su mayor fuerte, arte 10/10. En términos de gameplay es un juego simple en comparación a juegos como Hollow Knight o Dead Cells pero bastante divertido 8/10, en términos de historia esta es bastante confusa no es un juego que se cuente por si solo, si no te acompañas de una guía es básicamente imposible conseguir todo lo posible en su primera run, el juego en caso de necesitar platinarlo se necesitaría 2 hasta incluso una 3, 7/10. ¿El audio? buenísimo, juego que se recomienda jugar en su español natal 10/10.   Wena la wea de juego, esta precioso, gameplay entrete, el audio entero rico, la historia piola.

El primero de los Blasphemous es un juego que recomiendo definitivamente. Nunca había jugado a un Metroidvania y, sin embargo, el juego está al alcance de cualquiera que quiera empezar en el género. De cualquier manera, se siente que puede pulirse la fórmula, lo que se nota una vez se juega la segunda entrega. Aspectos como los objetivos no terminan de quedar claros y puede ser algo complicado orientarse en un principio, pero también reconozco que esto mismo incita al jugador a explorar el juego al máximo, adquiriendo distintos objetos y descubriendo Easter Eggs o misiones secundarias que harían que cualquiera se enamorase del espectacular aspecto artístico del juego: ya sean el arte, la música o la maravillosa cultura del sur de España que se respira, acompañada de un doblaje inolvidable, tanto en inglés o español.  En cuanto a la dificultad, puede que este sea uno de los apartados donde flaquee. Puede ser porque dentro de la propia libertad para explorar sea fácil sobrelevelear y dejar atrás el nivel esperado para algunos bosses sin darse cuenta. Esto aún así no impide que muchas de las peleas sean memorables y puedan dar dificultades a más de uno. En cuanto a la historia, si bien me parece que es uno de sus puntos fuertes, ya que cuando indagas en ella realmente se vuelve absorbente, no deja de ser complicada de seguir a lo largo del juego y al acabarlo queda una sensación de vacío que solo vas a poder suplir con algún video en Youtube que la analice. Sobre el contenido extra, este es maravilloso y amplio, además de gratuito con la compra. Incluye los típicos modos de juego con dificultad añadida y otros como un Boss Run. Mi único problema es, de nuevo, la dificultad para acceder a él sin alguna guía que lo facilite, pero puede ser que esto sea común en el género y más bien una preferencia mía. Finalmente, veo regularmente parches de mantenimiento y algo de contenido siendo añadido, los desarrolladores se ven muy implicados años después de su salida y otra entrega. En definitiva, recomiendo encarecidamente este juego, incluso jugarlo y completar seguidamente el 2. Una maravilla del género.

Vamos lá... O jogo é bom em vários sentidos. Seja pela gameplay, temática, fluidez trilha sonora, etc. Com relação a lore eu n posso falar muito pq sinceramente n entendi quase nada kkkkk. O jogo apresenta uma variedade bacana de inimigos e chefes, onde ambos possuem dificuldade desafiadora. Level design impecável. Arrisco a dizer que foi o melhor nesse quesito comparado aos outros metroidvania que joguei até essa analise.  Agora sobre as coisas que n gostei... eu acho que a coisa que mais me incomodou foi a quantidade de vezes que eu tive que rodar o mapa a pé. Eu falo isso pq o jogo só libera o sistema de frash travel se você fizer uma doação na igreja de 20000 lagrimas. É impossível alguem na primeira vez q esta jogando saber isso, a n ser que pesquise.   Além disso, o sistemas de quest desse jogo é muito complexo.... beleza que tem algumas q até da pr fazer de boas, mas tem outras que chega a ser ridículo o tanto de coisa que tem q fazer sem o jogo te dar dicas claras(ex: final verdadeiro) . Novamente, só pesquisando...  No mais é isso, tem esses problemas que me incomodaram, mas de resto o jogo é muito bom e vale a pena jogar. Super recomendo!

Blasphemous will remind you of a golden era in video gaming  One where things aren't perfect; mechanics aren't wrinkle free; and story-telling is a clear goal but may leave you still wondering WTF is going on.  Blasphemous is all of these, and an extraordinary bit of addicting gameplay and learned boss battling atop it all.   Will be checking out Blasphemous II once I 100% this bugger.  Devs: The time-run trials made me hate myself, my keyboard and you. All is now forgiven. You've been rid of your guilt.

A great experience. While the gameplay is nothing to really write home about, the game shines the most when it comes to building this twisted, dark, and depressing world. Everything about this world is oppressing and hostile in almost every aspect. It's so hostile that imagining living a "normal" life feels impossible. The lore of this game is so dark and messed up it's ridiculously absurd. I kept playing this game to continue to learn and see more about this world, the same way you'd keep reading a book nonstop because you're so immersed in it you can't put it down. I absolutely think you should play this game if you're a fan of good quality world building, gothic themes, and religious horror.

Pontos positivos: - Arte bonita e bem detalhada. - Bem ambientado. - Mapa fácil de se localizar e memorizar. - Não necessário grinding para ficar forte ou avançar na historia. - Mecânica fácil de acostumar. Pontos Negativos: - Hitbox inconsistente ( Personagem, projeteis e cenário).  - Inimigos do cenário dão mais desafios que os chefes. - Jogabilidade dura ( mas dá pra acostumar). - Em PT-BR alguns itens tem sua descrição cortada. ( mas acho que foi só um bug msm).  Achei o jogo legal e bem bonito, mas enjoei infelizmente não me agradou da forma que eu esperava. Achei o personagem meio pesado e meio duro de ser jogar, o hitbox me fez morrer varias vezes de bobeira em partes onde o penitente deveria agarrar ele simplesmente passou reto, projeteis que claramente não me acertaram mas tomei dano igual, enfrentei uns 5 ou 6 chefes e pouco me lembro qual deles eu de fato me senti desafiado.  Algumas descrições de itens são estranhas, vc pede a descrição e ela não te dá base pra saber se o item é bom ou é ruim. Parei um pouco depois de achar a mulher coxuda com tatuagem e não me sinto tentado a jogar dnv mas é um bom jogo, recomendo dar uma chance.

This game certainly requires patience.   I played the demo a while back and was captivated by the art, music, and the boss. So (when Blasphemous 1 and 2 + dlc were on sale) I wanted to come back and the play the full game, and here I am, making a review.   WEAPON:  I like the unique part where you only stick with one weapon throughout the whole game, and get abilities as you level up Mea Culpa (kinda reminds me of Hollow Knight.) Having too much weapon choice overwhelms me a little, in my opinion of course.  ART DIRECTION:  I love the cutscenes being strictly pixel art, unless I've been hiding under a rock I have never seen that before in a Metroidvania. Music is so fire, even when some tracks are in their own style, they have some sort of consistency involving dread or holiness.  GAMEPLAY:  Onto gameplay, there were some parts of the map that just reminded me of silksong with its difficulty. If you take [spoiler] Mourning and Havoc [/spoiler] for example, (in my opinion) that area started pissing me off with all the hard hitting enemies and electric obstacles to get through. Yeah, I might be slightly masochistic, but damn that area, and [spoiler] The Sleeping Canvases (with the spike pits and swinging axe things) [/spoiler] started pushing my buttons way too much. Any other area, I can get through comfortably with the right items equipped.  Speaking of items, any type of items, weather it be bones, or things to equip, are impossible to miss in Blasphemous. The games are dense in stuff to get. It encourages me to explore more around the map and find other stuff I need later on.  BOSSES:  If you know me, bosses are one of my favorite parts of any game! None of the bosses in Blasphemous 1 really disinterest me. Some are even iconic. My favorite bosses have to be [spoiler] Melquíades, Quirce, and Crisanta [/spoiler], but, what really brings down the experience is the Penitent One's sluggish movement, especially with healing, like omg, I'm glad they sped that up in Blasphemous 2, cause in the former, you can't move during healing, and when you choose a moment to heal during a boss (that's fast paced), you most likely get jumped.  PLOT:  I would include differences between Blasphemous 1 and 2's plot, but I barely played through the sequel, so I will hold that off for now. Speaking of Blasphemous 1, I might be stupid, not Spanish, nor religious in any way, but I kind of understood the plot? What I grasped from it was the Penitent One has to defeat the [spoiler] Miracle, or maker(s) of the Miracle [/spoiler]. It's pretty interesting of a plot.   I REALLY love the part where [spoiler] the Penitent One frees Crisanta from some type of metaphorical chains, and she helps us during phase three of His Holiness Escribar. I ate that up. [/spoiler]  What I dislike about Blasphemous 1, in terms of gameplay and plot, is that you can be permanently locked out of endings! I tried getting the true ending in my first playthrough, did everything btw except equip [spoiler] Apodictic Heart of Mea Culpa BEFORE fighting Crisanta [/spoiler], and I was locked out of the true ending, getting Ending A instead, and having to restart on a fresh save. Pretty frustrating, but I got over it since I got through my second playthrough in a measly 10 hours.  BLASPHEMOUS 2 DIFFERENCES:  Now, I'll keep Blasphemous 2 vague (I only played it for 30 minutes as of reviewing). I noticed changes immediately between the two games. Pros first, I LOVE the Penitent One's movement. He feels a bit weird in physics, but he's faster, and fast means faster healing. A selection of weapons was okay. I chose the fast jabber things, and will keep that throughout the whole playthrough. I am NOT switching.  That's all the pros I have, now to the cons (in my opinion). The music is kinda bland to me, not that it's bad, but it just doesn't remind me of Blasphemous? The art direction in cutscenes doesn't stick out to me. The fluidity and shading is gorgeous in the cutscenes, but that's it. It just reminds me of any other modern game cutscene. I'll keep bosses vague, I only played through one, and it was alright. I was indifferent to it, and only had fun testing out the Penitent One's movement, not the boss's attacks at all. I feel like the only bosses I would like is the final bosses, the skeleton one doing the 'Saturn eating is son' pose, and HOPEFULLY the dlc. All of it was just OKAY. Not bad at all, but okay.  What I certainly do not like about Blasphemous 2 is paying for the dlc. Like, I'm certain that Blasphemous 1's dlc was free? Why pay for dlc when it locks you out of the true ending? Weird choice. That's why I had to get them on sale. It's unfair of me to review Blasphemous 2 when I barely have gameplay of it, so I will make a review on that game when finished, and see if whatever I said above holds up.  CONCLUSION:  If you have patience like a Rain World player, and you like games with a grim art style, I recommended Blasphemous 1.

It just didn’t click for me, even though on paper it should have. The mix of genres and overall theme felt like something I’d normally enjoy and I’ve even tried getting into it more than once over time, but it still hasn’t really worked out.  The biggest problem is how it feels to actually play. Movement and controls come across as quite janky, which makes everything feel less smooth than it should. On top of that, the level design didn’t really hold my attention, areas tend to blend together and there’s not much that pulls me into wanting to explore further.  That said, I do like the game’s aesthetic direction and the world building has potential, the lore especially seems interesting and I can see why others might get drawn into it more than I did. Idk, maybe I’ll give it another proper attempt down the line and see if it changes for me, but for now I just can’t really get into it ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

Это абсолютный шедевр во всех смыслах этого слова. Околобиблейская атмосфера, сопровождающаяся превосходным саундтреком. Единственный момент: я настоятельно рекомендую проходить ее на геймпаде, в принципе как любую другую игру подобного жанра. Великолепие в чистом виде.

tem uma estética muita interessante, design das fases e inimigos foda, a história é muito boa também, tem algumas analogias e criticas ao cristianismo, especialmente no funcionamento do mesmo na Espanha. Não super desafiador, consideravelmente mais fácil que os Dark Souls, mas bem divertido, alguns bosses obrigatórios tu realmente vai se fuder, mas no geral a dificuldade mesmo é os opcionais.  A exploração pode ser um problema na primeira run, vc fica perdido, sem direção, então tem que estar com paciencia de certa forma, caso não queira procurar um guia no youtube (algo que eu não achei necessário, zerei sem guia algum), mas de fato pode ser mais demorado, mais estressante e pode te estimular a desistir do game pela preguiça de explorar e descobrir a posição dos boss, você tambem descobrirá os espinhos, e eles são bem frustrantes. Pixel art can push away people who don't know how to appreciate the work behind it. Being used to 3D in gaming can limit one's ability to value artistic expression through other techniques or concepts. However, beyond its visual art, Blasphemous shines for the authenticity of its story and characters, and for adopting the Soulslike formula within the Metroidvania genre—essentially, a 'Soulsvania.' For such a small team of developers to create this great game and blaze a trail is something very valuable.

Since Blasphemous was not compatible with the usual PS5 controller that I use, and playing with keyboard controls seemed like a terrible form of penance, I went out of my way to purchase a custom Xbox controller that is colored and named after my orange cat just for this game. And boy, how worth it this ordeal was. The game has a beautiful smoothly animated grotesque pixel art style based on Catholicism, the combat has a lot of great features and challenges that separate it from other games in the genre, the world is addicting to explore, the music is a sleeper-hit with a lot of catchy tunes, and the lore/story was captivating in more ways than one.  To start, the combat was a slight curveball since there was no pogoing, it has a parry system that begs to be used thanks to its sound design and brutal death animations, and dodging was less based on having a brief bit of invulnerability and more about moving out of the way of something at a lower position. I learned most of these things in the first area on the left side of the map. This clearly wasn’t the intended starting route. Unlike on the intended route, it forced me to learn to dodge, deflect, and wall jump to survive. There was also a ton of deadly platforming that taught me that spikes were an instant-kill. The route felt like a nice circle that offered plenty of short cuts. Despite my newly gained experience from that gauntlet, the Ten Piedad kicked my ass multiple times even with a healing assist from a helper. It was unfortunately one of the only few bosses in the base game that forced me to memorize its animations. Most bosses after this were unique and striking, but they were also too easy. I think the main reason for this is that I am a loot goblin and I explored the hell out of every area before I fought its boss. I probably also fought the bosses in the easiest order too. Luckily, the exploration is the highlight of these kinds of games and that holds true here too. I was addicted to collecting bones for an unknown reason, finding healing upgrades from a floating naked woman with swords piercing her (this is based on real scripture), collecting baby angels, uncovering rosaries that changed my gameplay style, and coming across healing and mana upgrades. It was a rush to loop back around and visit the boy with the old man slowly being birthed from his chest with every upgrade I got, along with the nurse that kisses wounds and the bone collector. It was interesting that upgrading flasks cost a use of one so I had to pace my upgrades with when I found empty flasks to install.  Anyway, I eventually approached the bridge to the endgame area after exploring all I could without looking up anything, and I faced off against Edras. He taught me parrying was a lot more useful than dodging and he forced me to slot in different rosaries exclusively for him. Little did I know that fighting an actually decent boss was the worst thing I could have done. I am a very patient man, especially when it comes to video games, but locking the DLC true ending behind the act of not facing a boss the game hypes you up for is horrendous. So I was forced to enter NG+ and play most of the game over again, all just to talk to a statue before fighting Edras. However, I will relent that NG+ was actually the most fun time I had with this game. Everything felt as truly difficult as I wished it was, and that encouraged me to get all of the collectables and strategize for all of the DLC bosses. Bosses who were originally pushovers like the three witches, Crisanta, and the flaming Quirce, all gave me a very hard time. Crisanta’s second phase required me to pay close attention to her fighting positions and have crazy reflexes. It was a truly great boss that I only defeated by subconsciously learning her patterns… and spamming the shield prayer. The true final boss wasn’t very hard, but I liked its spectacle and how it stripped you of all of your belongings. The bosses only relegated to NG+ were made to prepare you for the greatest one, who has the moves of every previous boss, so I beat it on my first try since every previous boss taught me well. I also liked the big guy with the horn twisted around his arm, he played a haunting tune.  Anyway, back to the present. The Mother of Mothers area was the coolest to explore thanks to libraries of ghosts and paintings, the bull painting, it connects to the lower parts of the game that that loops to the center of the map, and the enemies drop so many tears of atonement that it’s a cinch to get that shrine teleportation perk that costs 20K. I was having such a dandy time that I didn’t realize I had reached the final boss, who was disappointingly easy, but luckily the game accounts for this in more ways than one. When I first came back to gain the shrine teleportation ability, I also collected enough bones to fight Isidora, who was exactly who I was wishing for this whole time. She kicked my ass a stupendous amount of times even after I made a build specifically for her. On top of keeping me from pausing, she forced me to try out the long parry ability that came in clutch, but she was so overwhelming by the end of the battle and she had so much health that I always lost mere moments from victory. That is, until I didn’t and thanked the game for a nice challenge. Now, I want to shift my focus to the game’s art direction and story, which is one of a kind.   I want to start with the voice acting which seemed the most inspired by Dark Souls. A lot of people have deep powerful voices with a variety of accents that make you hear their unique struggles through their voices. While from the outside, much of the game seems overly grotesque and violent, but it turns out much of what we see is based on real Catholic scripture and artworks that date as far back as the 1800s. For example, after defeating the first boss, the penitent one takes blood pouring from its wound and drenches his helmet in it. This is based on how Jesus’s wound from a spear let out both water and blood, making this reference a form of baptism for a newly reborn warrior. Much of the game’s character designs are based on real world events such as the pointed capped folk that the penitent one was designed after, who tried to divert attention from them and towards God by covering themselves. His helmet also had a thorn crown along with the Mea Culpa’s hilt which are obvious references too. Some characters like the three faced woman resemble the crazed look of Saturn as he devoured one of his children, and the painted bull who was split open, deriving from two separate paintings, are among my favorites. However, there are plenty of new creations like the paraded skeleton who only works as a boss because of the hands that puppeteer it, the woman who was so severely burnt that she resembled a man and people worshiped her for it, and the poor boy who who had an old man climb out from his chest the more I interacted with him. All of these individuals, references or not, were heavily altered by the Miracle. The game keeps throwing us cruelties and seemingly compassionate acts that the Miracle bestowed upon people even at their own request, along with the hint that the Miracle can be offended based on the fates of the DLC bosses. The only way to learn why is through the true ending. Basically, the Miracle was manipulated by higher beings that humanity unknowingly wished into existence. This made me think about the deleted chapter of Berserk where Griffith met the Heart of Evil, who was also unknowingly brought to existence by humanity’s need for a reason for terrible things that happen to them. This along with the rosary, Crimson Heart of a Miura, makes me believe there is a clear correlation here. All of this is to say that this game has an extremely fascinating way of storytelling through its artwork that I haven’t seen in any other game. Blasphemous surprised me in many ways and I am looking forward to playing the sequel.

Blasphemous es una experiencia. Al ser el primero de su saga, se nota la mayoría de cosas en un estado que se mejoran en su siguiente entrega, y eso mismo, volviéndose en su sello de identidad pese a ser ambos del mismo universo. Este juego es más lento y pesado en el movimiento, no hay variedad de armas, y muchas cosas llegan a ser innecesarias pero no le quita lo divertido: todo lo que digo va acorde a la historia y narrativa tan cruda que presenta. Hago énfasis, no es que sea malo en lo técnico o lo jugable, simplemente es algo aún que falta por pulir. A mi me cambió la vida, amo el juego y se convirtió en mi favorito de todos los tiempos por encima de sagas como Dark Souls, The Legend of Zelda u otros indies aclamados. Tiene una identidad marcada: es como un libro de fantasía medieval con tono andaluz vuelto juego. Gracias, The Game Kitchen, entre los dos de esta hermosa experiencia, este es el mejor. Esperaré con gusto cuando saquen el tercero, tengo fe.

This game is good at world-building and storytelling, creating quite the captivating experience. The world and art design are amazing, featuring cleverly interconnected maps that can make exploration a joy, all tied together by a well composed soundtrack. However, the experience is hampered by its technical execution. The controls often feel sluggish and unpolished, noticeably during combat where especially dodging and dodge dashing lack the necessary smoothness. Because commands don't flow into each other naturally, the gameplay can become quite frustrating, and the platforming sections unfortunately leave a lot to be desired. It becomes particularly horrid during the Miriam Challenges. Oh, and there are also missable items due to infamously stupid "soulslike quests". It can be a nice journey if you have the patience for the clunky handling.  Update: It gets even worse. The slightest misstep during NPC quests locks you out of getting 100% completion in a single save file, which in turn locks you out of getting the respective achievement. So basically all the play time down the drain if you're an achievement hunter.  Deleted the game right after.

I walked through Cvstodia for 32 hours, and I’m still not ready to leave.  Blasphemous is not just a game, it feels like stepping into a twisted sermon. As a Spanish speaker, playing it in its original language feels right — every line of dialogue sounds like it’s being whispered by a priest in a forgotten church, telling you a story that was never meant to comfort you. The narrator, Deogracias… there’s something hauntingly beautiful about the way he speaks, like devotion turned into something heavy and eternal.  The art is, simply put, unforgettable. Every screen looks like a moving painting of suffering and faith. It doesn’t try to be pretty — it commits to its vision completely, and that’s what makes it so powerful.  In terms of gameplay, it finds a balance that many games struggle with. It carries the weight of a Dark Souls-like experience while still embracing the exploration and progression of a Hollow Knight-style world. It’s challenging, but not overly punishing — the kind of difficulty that asks for your attention, not your frustration.  Not everything is perfect. Some quests feel a bit obscure, almost too precise in what they expect from you. But honestly, a few minutes of searching is enough to get back on track, and it never really breaks the experience.  I’ve reached two endings so far, and knowing there’s still a “true” one waiting for me makes it even harder to put the game down. And yes, I’m already planning to dive into Blasphemous 2 after this.  I would recommend this game to anyone who enjoys deep exploration, hidden secrets, and the satisfaction of uncovering everything a world has to offer. But also to more casual players who simply want to experience something artistically unique.  This is not just a journey through a game.  It’s a pilgrimage.

Blasphemous es una obra maestra del género metroidvania que combina una brutal jugabilidad tipo dark souls con un impresionante pixel art inspirado en el folklore y la imaginería religiosa del barroco sevillano. Con un combate visceral y una atmósfera oscura y mística, ofrece una experiencia desafiante y única en la que exploras la retorcida tierra de Cvstodia como El Penitente.
This is real art. Although the game is called Blasphemous, it shows respect for religious beliefs everywhere.

Me ha sorprendido mucho, me esperaba un metroidvania entretenido en general y siento que he jugado algo increíble.   La exploración se premia mucho y me ha parecido muy satisfactorio, el sistema de combate no se me ha hecho repetitivo a pesar de no haber usado muchas combinaciones.   Los jefes son espectaculares y tienen su historia personal, la cual puedes descubrir al leer los objetos que te vas encontrado. Recomiendo hacerlo.   Los personajes y el lore son muy interesantes y me he quedado con ganas de más, así que tengo muchas ganas de jugar a su segunda parte.   La parte de las plataformas me ha gustado en general, aunque no es su punto fuerte. Los retos de Miriam han sido bastante duros en ese aspecto, aunque me dieron más problemas algunas colisiones "extrañas" que el control en sí.   En general, lo recomiendo mucho si te gusta la exploración, el lore interesante y los combates memorables. Es un juego buenísimo, de los mejor que he jugado últimamente, y que mis acais, si digo mentiras, se queden sin luz.

What a lovely game. Especially that I got it on a 90% sale, so time spent to money spent ratio is great. The mechanics are simple, but fun as hell, I did not mind doing 2,5 playthroughs to get all the achivs, and I still didn't do the post-launch content and the true ending. It's just, 40 hours of such simple gameplay gets boring eventually, I do believe I can easily squeeze out a dozen or so more hours of fun.  I have only three small complaints. First of all, platforming is sometimes clunky, Miriam's challenges was one of the most infuriating things I did these last few months as they're a time-limited platforming challenges. Second of all, the quests that have an in-game timer are extremely unintuitive and you can lose them without even knowing you started them. Lastly, there's somehow an unfixed bug where you can fall to your death in a way where you can't retrieve your own body and I suffered from that bug in both of my full playthroughs. Still, these are small nitpicks and annoyances, the game is great overall.

O famoso "Dark Souls" 2D, que vive para o hype!  A ambientação do jogo é incrível, a trilha sonora maravilhosa e quanto mais você joga mais curioso e perplexo você se encontra sobre o mundo ao redor.  Para um metroidvania, as opções de jogabilidade são bastante simples e até diria bem "limitadas", entrento, o jogo consegue cumprir bem ao que se propõe, e no momento que você se acostuma com a jogabilidade é muito prazeroso resolver os puzzles de plataforma e principalmente as boss fights, que são o ponto alto do jogo.  Um problema muito grande que você encontra nesse jogo é a falta de direção e o que fazer em determinados momentos e situações, até mesmo para avançar na missão principal. Eu entendo que é um dos tipos de jogos que não "dão a mão para o jogador", que eu gosto bastante, mas em determinados momentos acredito que o jogo passa dos limites, sendo necessário você ir atrás de guias para progredir no jogo e em algumas quests, é impossível você descobrir por si só.  Para chegar ao true ending do jogo por exemplo, é necessário que você cumpra determinadas missões EM ORDEM, mate alguns boss EM ORDEM para alcançar esse objetivo, algo que não é possivel na sua primeira jogatina, assim como outras quests que o jogo proporciona.  No geral, o saldo continua positivo, apesar de algumas mecanicas e decisões para progressão no jogo serem frustrantes, a luta contra os Boss, ambientação, trilha sonora e um universo rico em referências a cultura espanhola e Cristã, tornam Blasphemous em uma das melhores experiencias em que tive jogando um metroidvania, SUPER RECOMENDO!!!

Blasphemous é um jogo que vai te estressar, te humilhar e ainda vai te penalizar por isso. Mas meus amigos que jogo....Pra mim que não estou acostumado com jogos souslike, a dificuldade foi absurda. Os chefes são uns desgraçados e quando você finalmente vence sente um mix de orgulho e trauma.  O que me pegou muito foi a ambientação. A arte é absurda de linda, cheia de detalhes grotescos e religiosos que dão um peso único ao mundo de cvstodia. Um dos pixel art mais perfeito que já vi. Além disso a trilha sonora combina perfeitamente com a atmosfera do jogo e com cada parte diferente do mapa.  O mundo é muito rico tb, passa uma vibe que claramente os devs envolvidos tiveram um cuidado com isso. Eu sei que o jogo tem 2 finais, e mesmo não conseguindo fazer o final 'certo' sei que vou passar por toda aquela raiva de novo para conseguir.  Dito isso eu recomendo 100%, sem sombra de dúvida. Me vejo agora na obrigação de adquirir Blasphemous 2 para mais momentos de alegria e ódio gratuito.

美术完美融合了西班牙的风土民俗非常有特色，但游戏本体就是某种苦痛奇迹。 5/3 更新：设定太完美了！制作组对家乡的爱意简直溢出屏幕，天主教艺术题材很常见但是没有找到哪款游戏能比它更好融入一个地区的风土历史，虽然是架空游戏但从场景美术到即使是小怪的人物设计看完全就是安达卢西亚痛游。 缺点非常明显就是手感一般加部分地图设计幽默，橄榄枯萎之地和群峰墓地这俩能进博物馆的幽默地图我会记你们一辈子！入库多年一直没打完就是因为雪山地图卡关打得人红温，前期没有升级去雪山完全给自己找罪受，手残一个档玩了三十小时其中有13小时都是耗在雪山，但雪山周树人的支线和推图顺序强绑定又导致不得不去。虽说过了雪山之后就逐渐爽起来但雪山给我造成的阴影是不可磨灭的，如果这俩地图能优化一下这游戏从评分到销量会好上不少。
